title: IL и JIT: набор инструкций CLR и компиляция метода при первом вызове

cards.prompt: IL is a stack-based instruction set: a+b compiles to ldarg.0 (2) ldarg.1 (3) add (88=0x58) ret (42=0x2A) — push a, push b, add pops two & pushes the sum, ret. Expression-bodied gives clean IL with no nop, so GetILAsByteArray prints 2 3 88 42
cards.code: using System; using System.Reflection; class C { public static int Add(int a,int b)=>a+b; } var il = typeof(C).GetMethod("Add").GetMethodBody().GetILAsByteArray(); Console.WriteLine(string.Join(" ", il));

cards.prompt: Each source operator maps to one IL opcode: a*b compiles to the same stack shape as a+b but with mul (90=0x5A) in place of add (88=0x58), so it prints 2 3 90 42 — one byte differs from Add's 2 3 88 42
cards.code: using System; using System.Reflection; class C { public static int Mul(int a,int b)=>a*b; } var il = typeof(C).GetMethod("Mul").GetMethodBody().GetILAsByteArray(); Console.WriteLine(string.Join(" ", il));

cards.prompt: IL is a stack language, so a method body carries a MaxStackSize (evaluation-stack depth), not registers. For Add the body reports maxstack=8 (compiler headroom) and a 4-byte IL body, so it prints maxstack=8 il=4
cards.code: using System; using System.Reflection; class C { public static int Add(int a,int b)=>a+b; } var b = typeof(C).GetMethod("Add").GetMethodBody(); Console.WriteLine($"maxstack={b.MaxStackSize} il={b.GetILAsByteArray().Length}");
